claim: In older adults, high-contrast visual acuity is generally preserved, while visual performance under reduced contrast, reduced luminance, glare, and divided-attention conditions is substantially impaired.
support_summary: Multiple studies indicate that standard/high-contrast acuity often shows relatively low failure rates or remains within licensing/normal ranges in many older adults, while nonstandard measures reveal larger age-related deficits; for example, standard high-contrast acuity had the lowest failure rates compared with nonstandard tests in older adults (PMID: 12028258), many older people with good acuity were nevertheless impaired under low/changing light, glare, low contrast, or attentional demands (PMID: 11384002), and older adults with good acuity still showed reduced reading performance linked to low-contrast vision and attentional field integrity (PMID: 11384009). Several studies directly support impairment under reduced contrast, luminance, and glare: low-contrast acuity was worse in older adults despite equivalent conventional acuity (PMID: 3407728), contrast sensitivity declines substantially after age 60 even without major ocular pathology (PMID: 7158654), mesopic contrast sensitivity and glare sensitivity decline with age (PMID: 15290150), and glare markedly worsened nighttime driving performance in older drivers (PMID: 28564700). Evidence also supports divided-attention/processing deficits, with useful field of view impairment predicting crash risk and being mediated largely by divided-attention difficulty (PMID: 9546567), and attentional field measures being more informative than standard acuity in aging outcomes (PMID: 20642347). Population-based and functional studies further show that glare sensitivity, contrast sensitivity, and UFOV are often more predictive of disability, crashes, or mortality than acuity alone in older adults (PMID: 17389475, PMID: 11133849, PMID: 19832733). Some support items focus on disease-specific groups such as cataract, ARM, or diabetes rather than general aging (e.g., PMID: 16643756, PMID: 12093644, PMID: 24825104), so they are only indirect support for the broad claim.
contradict_summary: There is only one contradicting item, and its abstract does not truly refute the claim. In older cataract patients, contrast sensitivity and glare acuity were significantly worse than controls, while many eyes still had visual acuity >=0.5, leading the authors to argue for adding contrast and glare testing for older drivers (PMID: 10208259). This actually aligns with the claim's emphasis that acuity alone can miss substantial impairment under glare and reduced contrast, although it is disease-specific and does not establish that high-contrast acuity is generally preserved in all older adults. Thus, the contradiction category appears misclassified based on the abstract.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Across many studies, high-contrast acuity is often relatively preserved or less sensitive to aging than nonstandard measures, whereas reduced contrast, reduced luminance/night conditions, glare, and attentional/UFOV demands show clearer and more functionally important age-related impairment; the lone contradicting study is likely miscategorized and in fact is broadly consistent with the claim.

claim: In older adults, stereopsis, color discrimination, and peripheral visual field function are often markedly reduced.
support_summary: Several studies support age-related declines in stereopsis and color-related visual function in older adults. An optometric comparison found significant decrements in older versus middle-aged adults for stereopsis and color vision (PMID: 8355971), and a large longitudinal overview reported that many older people with good acuity still have difficulty with tasks involving stereopsis under everyday conditions (PMID: 11384002). A population study of adults over 58 reported that even very coarse stereopsis declines dramatically with age, although this decline was not seen among those selected for good ocular function, suggesting ocular rather than purely cortical causes (PMID: 11100891). Additional evidence points to age-related reductions in color-related performance, including declines in recognizability of colored targets from about age 50 (PMID: 8043155) and worsening cone-mediated color contrast sensitivity after about age 40 (PMID: 32596723). For peripheral function, older adults showed substantially reduced peripheral visual acuity (PMID: 2622675), and visual-field mapping across the lifespan found age-related increases in perimetric thresholds that were more pronounced in the periphery (PMID: 22484795), though some included studies are only indirect or use related rather than identical measures of the claim.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple abstracts consistently indicate age-related reductions in stereopsis, color-related vision, and peripheral visual performance, although support for peripheral visual field function is somewhat indirect and the evidence does not always establish that these reductions are uniformly 'marked' in all older adults.

claim: Across multiple spatial vision measures in older adults, visual performance declines with age at a similar overall rate, although different visual functions begin to decline at different ages.
support_summary: Several population-based and normative studies report that multiple aspects of visual function worsen with age in older adults, including acuity, contrast sensitivity, glare-related performance, visual fields, and mesopic/scotopic measures (PMID: 9071208, PMID: 11345345, PMID: 17397740, PMID: 35979702). Some evidence specifically supports different onset ages for decline across functions: for example, stereoacuity remained stable until the mid-70s before accelerating downward while other measures declined more linearly in the Salisbury Eye Evaluation Study (PMID: 9071208), mesopic/glare sensitivity was stable until about 50 then declined (PMID: 15290150), and photopic spatial vision stayed age-invariant to about 50 whereas mesopic thresholds worsened from around 30 onward (PMID: 35979702). Additional studies found age-related losses in contrast sensitivity or functional visual acuity and declines beginning around 40-60 years depending on the task (PMID: 7158654, PMID: 9008645, PMID: 32596723, PMID: 21584727). However, the support for the specific phrase "similar overall rate" is uneven because many abstracts show age-related decline without directly comparing rates across measures. There is also an internal discrepancy within the supporting set: the SKI overview explicitly states that rates of decline vary widely across vision functions, which weakens direct support for the claim's similarity-of-rate component (PMID: 11384002).
contradict_summary: The strongest contradictory theme is that aging effects are not uniform across visual functions or tasks. The SKI overview, although placed in the support set, directly states that rates of decline with age vary widely across different vision functions, which conflicts with the claim's "similar overall rate" wording (PMID: 11384002). Experimental studies also show differential age effects by stimulus type: aging affected sensitivity to polar versus linear gratings differently, rather than at a common rate (PMID: 24141613), and one motion study found older adults could outperform younger adults for small-aperture global motion stimuli, arguing against a general monotonic decline across all visual measures (PMID: 25152731). One repeatability study suggests only modest average loss across the normal lifespan and does not show increasing variability with age, which only weakly challenges the claim because it still reports some loss on each test (PMID: 10966067).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is moderately supported: many studies consistently show age-related decline across multiple visual functions, and several indicate that different functions begin declining at different ages. However, evidence for a truly similar overall rate across measures is mixed, with some abstracts explicitly indicating that decline rates differ substantially by visual function.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, myopia prevalence increased with age during childhood and adolescence, reaching very high levels in older adolescents.
support_summary: Multiple Taiwan-based population surveys directly support an age-related rise in myopia prevalence during childhood and adolescence, with especially high rates in older teens. A nationwide 2000 survey reported prevalence increasing from 20% at age 7 to 61% at 12, 81% at 15, and 84% at 16-18 years, with substantial high myopia by age 18 (PMID: 11760374). A 20-year review of nationwide surveys showed the same pattern, including 84% prevalence among 16-18-year-olds in 2000 and worsening refractive status with age (PMID: 15008558), and a later review of 1983-2017 survey data identified older age as a major risk factor and documented steadily increasing prevalence across age groups (PMID: 32679159). Additional regional Taiwan studies also found significantly higher myopia prevalence in older students or with increasing age, including rural Chia-Yi, suburban Chiayi County, and mountain aboriginal children, though some are more limited in age range or methodology (PMID: 32758194, PMID: 30120433, PMID: 18364272). A few listed studies are only indirectly supportive or less well matched to the claim because they do not specifically report childhood-to-late-adolescence prevalence trends in Taiwan, such as a non-Taiwan suburban study and an optical-components study focused on refraction rather than prevalence (PMID: 19624963, PMID: 2853544).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The strongest evidence comes from large nationwide Taiwanese surveys that explicitly show myopia prevalence rising with age through childhood and adolescence and reaching very high levels in older adolescents, with little to no direct contradictory evidence presented.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, girls had a higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia than boys.
support_summary: Direct support comes from a large nationwide Taiwan survey showing that girls had both higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia than boys, with mean refraction at age 18 of -4.12 D in girls versus -3.15 D in boys and high myopia prevalence of 24% versus 18% (PMID: 11760374). Additional Taiwan evidence is indirect but consistent: a national interview survey found higher self-reported myopia rates in females than males under age 40, though it did not focus specifically on schoolchildren or severity (PMID: 22344187). Several non-Taiwan Chinese studies also report higher myopia prevalence or more myopic refractive status in girls, including Beijing adolescents, Wenzhou children, Guangzhou twins, and a nationwide adolescent sample, supporting the general sex pattern but not the exact Taiwan-specific claim (PMID: 28606071, PMID: 27531112, PMID: 26011966, PMID: 35712300). Overall, the strongest abstract-level evidence is the Taiwan nationwide cycloplegic survey, while the rest are mostly indirect contextual support.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting items are from non-Taiwan populations and therefore only weakly challenge the Taiwan-specific claim. Two U.S. studies found no significant gender difference in refractive error or amount of myopia among children, although they did report biometric sex differences such as shorter axial length in girls (PMID: 11818372, PMID: 12637834). A Saudi clinic-based study reported myopia was more prevalent in boys than girls, but its setting, sampling, and population differ substantially from Taiwanese schoolchildren (PMID: 30524756). One item appears miscategorized as contradiction because it actually reports lower myopia prevalence in males, implying higher prevalence in females and thus partial support rather than contradiction; it also does not address severity (PMID: 19624963).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is driven mainly by a directly relevant nationwide Taiwan schoolchildren survey that explicitly found higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia in girls, while the contradicting evidence is mostly indirect, from other countries, and includes at least one likely miscategorized study.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, greater myopia severity was associated with longer axial length, and the study authors concluded that the rising prevalence and severity of high myopia may be related to earlier onset of myopia.
support_summary: Direct Taiwan schoolchild evidence strongly supports the claim: a nationwide 2000 survey found that axial length increased with myopia progression and concluded that increasing prevalence/severity, especially in younger groups, implied that preventing early-onset myopia might reduce severity (PMID: 11760374). A 20-year Taiwan trend analysis similarly reported that myopia onset shifted earlier (from age 11 to 8) while prevalence and high myopia increased, and concluded that worsening severity was due to younger onset (PMID: 15008558). Additional Taiwan data in teenagers showed axial length closely paralleled degree of myopia (PMID: 2853544), and several non-Taiwan or broader studies also consistently linked more severe myopia with longer axial length and younger onset with later high myopia (PMID: 27350183; PMID: 9639836; PMID: 26011966; PMID: 18296925). Some supporting items are only indirectly relevant because they involve adults, anisometropia, treatment studies, or non-Taiwan populations rather than Taiwanese schoolchildren specifically, but they generally align with the claimed axial length and early-onset patterns.
contradict_summary: One study in a special population of preterm schoolchildren with laser-treated retinopathy of prematurity found severe myopia despite no axial length difference versus controls, attributing myopia mainly to thicker lens, steeper cornea, and shallower anterior chamber rather than axial elongation (PMID: 23601812). However, this is a highly specific clinical subgroup and does not directly address typical schoolchildren in Taiwan or the authors' conclusion about earlier onset driving rising high myopia prevalence. Thus, it is a limited contextual contradiction rather than a strong refutation of the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from Taiwan school-based surveys directly showing that greater myopia is associated with longer axial length and that earlier onset likely contributed to rising prevalence and severity of high myopia, whereas the lone contradictory study concerns an atypical preterm ROP population with different biometric mechanisms.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, smoking and hypertension were associated with a higher likelihood of age-related macular degeneration, supporting smoking avoidance and hypertension prevention as potential strategies to reduce risk.
support_summary: Supporting studies consistently link cigarette smoking with higher risk, incidence, or progression of AMD, including several AREDS analyses showing smoking associated with neovascular AMD or progression to advanced AMD (PMID: 15808240, 24385141, 16816528, 21139980). A subset of supporting studies also report positive associations between hypertension or higher blood pressure and AMD outcomes, including neovascular AMD, exudative AMD, or incident ARM/AMD (PMID: 17953990, 9893587, 12939290, 24460653, 18222193, 26950426, 31595025, 3947296, 9194725). Several abstracts suggest dose-response patterns for smoking and note lower risk after smoking cessation, reinforcing the prevention implication for smoking avoidance (PMID: 8859077, 16361672, 8953988, 8827966). However, much of the support is indirect to the exact claim because many studies are outside AREDS, focus on progression/late AMD rather than overall AMD likelihood, or support smoking but not hypertension. Overall, the support side shows a strong recurring theme for smoking and a moderate but less uniform theme for hypertension/blood pressure.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes multiple studies reporting no significant association of smoking and/or hypertension with AMD or ARM, especially for hypertension: for example no predictive effect of smoking or hypertension on fellow-eye progression in SST (PMID: 19734762), no association with hypertension or smoking in a Brazilian cross-sectional study (PMID: 15905946), and no association of blood pressure/hypertension with AMD in SHIP-Trend (PMID: 31446436). Several population-based studies specifically found hypertension or blood pressure not associated with incident or prevalent AMD/ARM (PMID: 11471092, 8643241, 8460013, 17275090), while some others found smoking associated but hypertension not associated, creating only partial contradiction to the combined claim (PMID: 20547953, 9596493, 26644880). One item appears only weakly relevant because it concerns small hard drusen rather than AMD itself, so its contradiction is indirect (PMID: 36548342). Overall, the contradiction is driven mainly by inconsistent evidence for hypertension, whereas contradiction of smoking is less consistent because several “contradicting” studies still support smoking as a risk factor.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence for smoking as a higher-risk factor for AMD is strong and recurrent, including within AREDS-related analyses, while evidence for hypertension is mixed but supported by several studies; the main uncertainty is that hypertension associations are less consistent across cohorts than smoking.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, hyperopia, lens opacities, less education, female sex, increased body mass index, and white race were also associated with age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: Several studies support parts of the claim, especially associations of AMD with white race, hyperopia, lens opacity/cataract, lower education, and higher BMI. Within AREDS specifically, a prospective analysis found white race associated with incident neovascular AMD, and less education plus obesity associated with central geographic atrophy, but it did not identify hyperopia, lens opacities, or female sex as significant factors in the abstract (PMID: 15808240). Outside AREDS, case-control and population studies reported hyperopia and lens opacities/cataract as AMD risk factors (PMID: 9893587, PMID: 6881127, PMID: 7125436), hyperopia and lower education as associated with age-related maculopathy (PMID: 16774957), and higher BMI/obesity as associated with AMD incidence or progression in some cohorts (PMID: 12796248, PMID: 24667857). Multiple studies also consistently found higher AMD prevalence in white versus black populations (PMID: 10366070, PMID: 18268216, PMID: 11190014, PMID: 687553). A discrepancy is that many supporting studies only address subsets of the claimed factors or different populations/settings rather than reproducing the full AREDS finding.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly challenges specific components rather than the whole claim. One Indian population-based study found no direct association between AMD presence/severity and obesity indices, arguing against the BMI component, though in a different population from AREDS (PMID: 29941743). A Brazilian cross-sectional study found no association with hyperopia or race, but did observe associations with female sex, cataract, and obesity, making it mixed rather than wholly contradictory (PMID: 15905946). Another study linked small hard drusen to lower BMI and hyperopia, but explicitly stated these drusen are not an early manifestation of AMD, so its relevance to contradiction of AMD risk factors is indirect and somewhat miscategorized (PMID: 36548342).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support is strongest for white race, higher BMI, lower education, and hyperopia/lens opacity associations across AREDS-related or broader AMD studies, while contradiction is sparse, population-specific, and mainly limited to the BMI or hyperopia components rather than the full claim.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, geographic atrophy was associated with thyroid hormone use and antacid use, and large drusen or extensive intermediate drusen were associated with arthritis and hydrochlorothiazide use.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: A large matched case-control study found that, after adjustment, thyroid hormone use and thiazide diuretics were not associated with AMD overall, while antacid use showed only a small residual association that the authors suggested might reflect confounding rather than a true drug effect (PMID: 17544775). Another AREDS analysis examined geographic atrophy enlargement rate rather than baseline presence of GA or drusen phenotypes; although thyroid hormone use was associated in univariable analysis, only cholesterol-lowering medication use and diuretic use remained independently associated in multivariable models, so this does not directly support the specific claim and is misclassified as purely contradicting evidence (PMID: 34489337). Neither contradicting study addresses the claim's reported associations of large drusen/extensive intermediate drusen with arthritis and hydrochlorothiazide use. Overall, the main contradictory theme is lack of robust adjusted evidence for thyroid hormone or thiazide associations with AMD, with some ambiguity for antacids and for GA progression versus GA presence.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported based on the provided evidence. There is no direct supporting evidence here, and the strongest available study argues against adjusted associations of thyroid hormone and thiazide use with AMD, while the second study addresses GA progression rather than the specific cross-sectional phenotype associations in the claim.

claim: In patients with subfoveal choroidal neovascularization caused by age-related macular degeneration, verteporfin photodynamic therapy reduced the risk of moderate or severe vision loss compared with sham treatment over two years.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the TAP randomized placebo-controlled trials, which reported that over 24 months, 53% of verteporfin-treated eyes versus 38% of placebo-treated eyes lost fewer than 15 letters, indicating less moderate/severe vision loss overall, with the clearest benefit in predominantly classic lesions (59% vs 31%) (PMID: 11176980). Additional randomized evidence in minimally classic lesions also found lower rates of at least 3-line vision loss at 24 months with verteporfin versus placebo, especially in the reduced-fluence arm, though benefit was less uniform across dosing regimens (PMID: 15824216). Related TAP analyses and adjunct functional studies showed concordant benefits in contrast sensitivity, central visual field/scotoma progression, and angiographic control of CNV/leakage, all consistent with preserved visual function versus placebo/sham (PMID: 12441717, PMID: 15121371, PMID: 12867383). Several other items are only indirect or lower-quality support because they are 1-year studies, observational/historical-control studies, reviews, or guideline/commentary papers rather than direct 2-year sham-controlled evidence (PMID: 10532441, PMID: 15197060, PMID: 17300567). A notable discrepancy within the support set is that some abstracts emphasize that routine benefit was best established for predominantly classic lesions and less certain for minimally classic lesions in TAP, so support is strongest for selected lesion subtypes rather than all subfoveal AMD CNV uniformly (PMID: 11176980, PMID: 10532441).
contradict_summary: The main direct contradiction is a randomized placebo-controlled trial in occult-with-no-classic subfoveal CNV that found no statistically significant reduction in loss of at least 15 or 30 letters at 12 or 24 months with verteporfin versus placebo, suggesting lesion subtype strongly modifies efficacy (PMID: 19530976). Most other contradicting studies are weaker for this exact claim because they are noncomparative/retrospective cohorts, compare verteporfin with other active treatments rather than sham, or involve different lesion locations such as extrafoveal CNV or special subtypes such as RAP; these generally report poor outcomes or infer limited benefit of PDT in practice (PMID: 16010552, PMID: 22648253, PMID: 17021319, PMID: 16525824, PMID: 31918701). The ranibizumab comparison does not directly refute benefit versus sham; it shows verteporfin is inferior to anti-VEGF therapy, which is a different question (PMID: 17021319). Likewise, extrafoveal CNV and RAP studies are not well matched to the claim focused on subfoveal AMD CNV in sham-controlled trials, so their contradictory value is limited (PMID: 16010552, PMID: 16525824). Overall, the contradiction centers less on a universal lack of effect and more on evidence that verteporfin benefit is absent or small in certain AMD CNV subtypes, especially occult/no-classic lesions (PMID: 19530976).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. High-quality 2-year randomized placebo-controlled evidence directly shows verteporfin reduced the risk of moderate vision loss in subfoveal AMD CNV, especially for predominantly classic lesions, but contradictory evidence indicates that this benefit was not consistent across all lesion subtypes, particularly occult/no-classic CNV.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and occult-with-no-classic choroidal neovascularization, verteporfin photodynamic therapy produced better visual outcomes than sham treatment, including less loss of visual acuity and less progression of neovascularization.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is mostly indirect rather than a direct sham comparison. Exploratory analyses from TAP/VIP suggest verteporfin benefit in occult-with-no-classic lesions was greater for smaller lesions, with smaller treated lesions losing less vision than larger treated lesions and lesion size predicting treatment benefit (PMID: 12967792). A single-arm occult-only study reported that after 12 months, 82.4% lost <30 letters, 61.8% lost <15 letters, and fluorescein leakage fell substantially, supporting visual stabilization and reduced CNV leakage but without a sham arm (PMID: 18583995). A placebo-controlled ICGA subset study showed verteporfin arrested CNV growth and reduced leakage versus placebo, supporting less neovascular progression, though it was not specific to occult-only lesions and did not directly emphasize visual acuity outcomes (PMID: 12867383). Other cited studies/reviews are lower relevance or indirect because they are retrospective, non-comparative, or involve different lesion types such as PCV, despite generally describing verteporfin-associated visual stabilization in AMD (PMID: 17300567, PMID: 17891004, PMID: 14968084).
contradict_summary: The strongest directly relevant evidence is a randomized placebo-controlled trial in occult-with-no-classic CNV showing no statistically significant visual benefit of verteporfin over placebo at 12 or 24 months: 37% vs 45% lost >=15 letters at 12 months and 47% vs 53% at 24 months, with similarly small nonsignificant differences for >=30-letter loss (PMID: 19530976). This directly conflicts with the claim that verteporfin produced better visual outcomes than sham treatment in this population. Additional contradiction is weaker and more indirect: a comparative case series found PDT eyes had significantly worse final visual acuity than baseline, but it did not use sham and mixed CNV subtypes (PMID: 22648253). Another small case series in vascularized pigment epithelial detachment found frequent angiographic failure and poor visual outcomes, but it addresses a different AMD subgroup and is only indirectly contradictory (PMID: 17017194).
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more contradicted than supported. Although several studies suggest verteporfin can reduce leakage/CNV growth and may stabilize vision in some AMD patients, the most directly relevant sham-controlled evidence in occult-with-no-classic CNV found no significant visual advantage over placebo, which outweighs the mainly indirect supportive evidence.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and occult-with-no-classic choroidal neovascularization, the benefit of verteporfin photodynamic therapy was greater in eyes with smaller lesions or poorer baseline visual acuity than in eyes with larger lesions or better baseline visual acuity.
support_summary: The strongest directly relevant support comes from an exploratory analysis of TAP/VIP data showing that, in occult-with-no-classic CNV, verteporfin treatment benefit interacted significantly with lesion size: smaller lesions had less vision loss, while treatment-by-baseline visual acuity interaction was not significant, so support is strong for lesion size but not for poorer baseline acuity (PMID: 12967792). Several observational or mixed-lesion studies also found smaller baseline CNV/GLD predicted better or less poor outcomes after verteporfin PDT, including occult lesions in some analyses (PMID: 18301052, PMID: 17539784, PMID: 15606458, PMID: 16525824). However, multiple studies reported the opposite pattern for baseline vision—better baseline visual acuity predicted better final outcome after PDT rather than greater benefit in eyes with poorer baseline acuity (PMID: 18301052, PMID: 16187598, PMID: 14962414), which is a discrepancy within the purported support set. Other cited studies involve predominantly classic CNV or different retreatment regimens rather than the claim’s occult-with-no-classic population, so they offer only indirect support for the smaller-lesion part of the claim (PMID: 16225928, PMID: 18166408).
contradict_summary: The lone contradicting study is a small pilot of combination verteporfin PDT plus ranibizumab, not verteporfin monotherapy, in mixed neovascular AMD; it reported that lesion type and age had no influence on outcome, but the abstract does not clearly analyze lesion size or baseline visual acuity as modifiers of treatment benefit (PMID: 18779720). As such, it is only weakly contradictory and is not closely matched to the claim’s population or intervention. More importantly, some studies placed in the support group actually conflict with the claim’s baseline-visual-acuity component by associating better, not poorer, baseline vision with better outcomes after PDT (PMID: 18301052, PMID: 16187598, PMID: 14962414).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, mainly because the most relevant TAP/VIP exploratory analysis directly indicates greater verteporfin benefit in occult-with-no-classic CNV when lesions are smaller (PMID: 12967792). However, the claim’s added assertion about poorer baseline visual acuity is not well supported and is partly contradicted by several abstracts showing better baseline visual acuity predicts better outcomes.

claim: The Age-Related Eye Disease Study fundus-photograph grading system showed satisfactory reliability for detecting onset of advanced age-related macular degeneration in adults with age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: One study provides indirect but relevant support that the AREDS grading approach is reproducible: AREDS2 digital fundus photograph grading, based on a protocol only slightly modified from the original AREDS system, showed substantial agreement in repeat grading, with 91% and 96% of replicate grades within two steps on the AREDS severity scale and weighted kappas of 0.73 and 0.76; historical AREDS film grading also showed high reproducibility (88% within two steps, weighted kappa 0.88) (PMID: 23620429). This supports satisfactory reliability of the grading system framework for AMD assessment. However, the abstract focuses on reproducibility of lesion and severity-scale grading rather than directly testing reliability for detecting the onset of advanced AMD, so support is partial rather than definitive.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted, but only modestly. The available evidence indicates good reproducibility of the AREDS/AREDS2 fundus-photograph grading procedures, yet it does not directly demonstrate reliability specifically for detecting incident advanced AMD onset.

claim: The Age-Related Eye Disease Study fundus-photograph grading system showed moderate to substantial agreement for grading a range of age-related macular degeneration abnormalities, including pigment epithelial detachment, retinal hemorrhage or exudates, geographic atrophy, pigment changes, and drusen characteristics.
support_summary: Two studies provide indirect support that standardized fundus-photograph grading for AMD lesions is reproducible. A photographic comparison study reported exact agreement of 72% to 89% across lesions such as drusen, retinal pigment epithelial degeneration/pigment change, geographic atrophy, and exudative AMD, with kappa values ranging from 0.33 to 0.60, suggesting variable but often moderate agreement, though it did not specifically evaluate the AREDS grading system itself (PMID: 1454343). An AREDS2 reproducibility study found substantial agreement for the AREDS-derived severity scale, with weighted kappas of 0.73 and 0.76 for repeat grading and similar reproducibility to historical AREDS film grading, but it focused more on the severity scale than on each individual lesion named in the claim (PMID: 23620429). Overall, the support is mainly from related reproducibility data rather than direct abstract-level confirmation of all listed abnormalities in the original AREDS grading system.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but the evidence here is indirect. The available abstracts support reproducibility and moderate-to-substantial agreement for AMD photographic grading in AREDS-related settings, yet they do not directly and completely verify the full list of abnormalities in the specific original AREDS grading-system claim.

claim: The Age-Related Eye Disease Study classification system for age-related macular degeneration was suitable for longitudinal multicenter studies because grading showed little temporal drift over repeated evaluations.
support_summary: Direct support comes from an AREDS2 reproducibility study showing little temporal drift in repeated masked regrading: 91% of images were within two steps of baseline on the AREDS severity scale, and historical AREDS film-based grading showed similar performance (88% within two steps), with no significant AREDS2-AREDS difference in temporal-drift concordance (PMID: 23620429). This directly supports the suitability of the AREDS/AREDS-derived grading approach for longitudinal evaluation, including continuity across study eras and reading-center procedures. A second study found the AREDS simplified severity scale classified late-AMD risk similarly in a population-based cohort and the AREDS clinical-trial cohort over 5 and 10 years, supporting robustness and external validity, though it does not directly test temporal drift or repeated grading reliability (PMID: 27378016). Overall, the main supporting theme is reproducibility and stability of the AREDS classification framework, with one study being directly on temporal drift and one only indirectly relevant.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported. The strongest evidence directly demonstrates little temporal drift in repeated AREDS/AREDS2 grading and comparable reproducibility across historical and updated methods (PMID: 23620429), while the additional study offers only indirect support through long-term robustness of the classification system (PMID: 27378016).

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization who are not eligible for laser or photodynamic therapy, subretinal membrane excision with simultaneous autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation improves visual acuity more often than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a retrospective comparison showing that after CNV membrane excision, autologous RPE transplantation produced significantly more visual improvement (about 58-63% gaining at least 2 lines) than membrane excision alone (23.5%) in hemorrhagic neovascular AMD, with significant ETDRS gains in the transplant groups (PMID: 27496526). Additional uncontrolled case series of autologous RPE transplantation reported meaningful postoperative visual acuity gains, including 57% improving by at least 2 lines after combined subretinal surgery plus autologous RPE transplantation in foveal CNV from AMD (PMID: 11812425), and improved mean ETDRS scores after autologous RPE-Bruch's membrane complex transplantation in hemorrhagic AMD (PMID: 19117919). Another small case series also found significant postoperative visual improvement after autologous full-thickness RPE/choroid patch translocation following CNV removal (PMID: 31454718). One supporting item is less directly relevant because it used autologous iris pigment epithelium rather than RPE and lacked a comparator, though it reported mostly stable or improved acuity after membrane removal plus transplantation (PMID: 11030816).
contradict_summary: Several studies reported that adding pigment epithelial transplantation did not significantly improve visual function, though many of these involved iris pigment epithelium (IPE) or allogeneic rather than autologous RPE, which makes them only partially aligned with the claim. A study of adult human allogeneic RPE sheet transplantation at the time of subfoveal membranectomy found no significant improvement in visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, or reading speed at 1 year (PMID: 17303061), and a prospective study of autologous peripheral RPE translocation found no measurable functional benefit with frequent proliferative vitreoretinopathy and retinal detachment (PMID: 14693786). Multiple IPE-based studies similarly found preserved or stabilized vision without significant visual acuity improvement after membrane removal plus transplantation, including short-term and 1-year follow-up reports, one of which included a nonrandomized membrane-excision-only control and found no significant advantage (PMID: 11011682; PMID: 15300442; PMID: 10896035). A review-style article also summarized that IPE transplantation may stabilize acuity and reduce recurrence but does not improve vision (PMID: 15300389).
conclusion: The claim is overall somewhat supported, because one study directly comparing membrane excision with versus without autologous RPE transplantation found significantly more frequent visual improvement with transplantation (PMID: 27496526), and several uncontrolled autologous RPE series also reported postoperative acuity gains. However, the evidence is mixed because several negative studies exist, many of which involve IPE or allogeneic RPE rather than the exact autologous RPE intervention, so applicability to the precise claim is imperfect.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization, subretinal membrane excision with simultaneous autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation produces better reading acuity and higher multifocal electroretinography response density than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: One retrospective study reported that after choroidal neovascular membrane excision for neovascular AMD with massive subretinal hemorrhage, autologous RPE transplantation was associated with significantly greater ETDRS visual acuity improvement than excision alone, with about 58-63% gaining at least two lines versus 23.5% in the excision-only group (PMID: 27496526). However, this study did not specifically report reading acuity or multifocal electroretinography response density, so it only indirectly supports the claim. It also involved broader visual outcomes and somewhat different clinical context than the claim, limiting direct comparability.
contradict_summary: Several abstracts report little or no functional benefit from pigment epithelium transplantation at the time of subfoveal membrane removal. In a case series of allogeneic adult RPE sheet transplantation, best-corrected visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and reading speed did not improve significantly at 1 year, and complications were common (PMID: 17303061). A prospective study of autologous peripheral RPE translocation found no measurable positive functional effect, with stable vision in most patients, deterioration in some, and frequent retinal detachment (PMID: 14693786). A review of iris pigment epithelium transplantation states that such transplantation may stabilize vision and reduce recurrence but generally does not improve visual acuity (PMID: 15300389); however, this is a related but not identical intervention, so it is only indirectly contradictory. None of these contradicting items addresses multifocal ERG response density directly, creating a discrepancy between the claim's specific endpoints and the available evidence.
conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. The only supporting study shows better general visual acuity with autologous RPE transplantation versus excision alone, but it does not address the specific outcomes of reading acuity or multifocal ERG, whereas multiple studies report no clear functional improvement and substantial complications after pigment epithelium transplantation.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization, autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation appears to be a beneficial adjunct to membrane excision rather than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: Most supporting studies are uncontrolled case series of autologous RPE/RPE-choroid grafting performed after CNV membrane excision, and many report visual improvement or stabilization plus evidence of graft viability/perfusion on imaging and microperimetry. The strongest direct comparative evidence is a retrospective review showing significantly higher rates of ≥2-line visual improvement with autologous RPE grafts than with CNV excision alone (62.5% and 58.0% vs 23.5%) in neovascular/hemorrhagic AMD (PMID: 27496526). Several prospective or pilot series also found meaningful postoperative gains or stabilization after combined surgery, such as 57% gaining ≥2 lines without CNV recurrence in a 14-eye pilot study (PMID: 11812425), improvement in ETDRS scores after RPE-Bruch’s membrane transplantation (PMID: 19117919), and long-term reports suggesting maintenance of macular function and fixation on the graft for years (PMID: 21907969; PMID: 17562066). However, many of these studies do not directly compare against membrane excision alone, so their support for the specific comparative claim is indirect. Some “supporting” items are only mechanistic, review-based, or involve iris pigment epithelium rather than retinal pigment epithelium (PMID: 11030816, PMID: 12239351, PMID: 15300389, PMID: 11950236), which weakens their relevance and represents a categorization discrepancy.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes a directly relevant prospective noncontrolled study in eight patients reporting that autologous peripheral RPE translocation after membrane extraction had no measurable positive functional effect and a high proliferative vitreoretinopathy/retinal detachment rate (PMID: 14693786). Another interventional case series found that allogeneic adult RPE sheet transplantation at membranectomy did not significantly improve visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, or reading speed at 1 year, despite apparent graft tolerance under immunosuppression (PMID: 17303061), though this is less directly relevant because the claim concerns autologous transplantation. A nonrandomized comparison of autologous iris pigment epithelium transplantation versus membrane removal alone found no significant functional advantage over controls (PMID: 10896035), but this also differs from the claim because it used iris rather than retinal pigment epithelium. Overall, the contradicting studies emphasize lack of clear functional benefit in some series and substantial surgical complication risk, but two of the three are only partially on-target for the exact autologous RPE claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from multiple clinical series showing postoperative visual benefit and graft function, including one study directly suggesting better outcomes than membrane excision alone (PMID: 27496526), whereas contradictory evidence is smaller and partly less directly relevant because it involves allogeneic RPE or iris pigment epithelium rather than autologous retinal pigment epithelium.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration treated with intravitreal ranibizumab, an optical coherence tomography-guided variable-dosing regimen improved visual acuity and reduced central retinal thickness.
support_summary: Direct support comes from prospective OCT-guided variable-dosing ranibizumab studies showing improved vision and reduced retinal thickness, especially the 2-year PrONTO study, where mean visual acuity improved by 11.1 letters and OCT central retinal thickness fell by 212 µm (PMID: 19376495). Other PRN/treat-and-extend or flexible OCT-monitored cohorts likewise reported significant gains in visual acuity and decreases in central retinal or macular thickness, including 24-month case series and real-world studies (PMID: 19217019, PMID: 20591490, PMID: 28449645, PMID: 19730802, PMID: 21459217). Several additional studies found the same pattern after OCT-guided retreatment or individualized dosing, though often without a comparator and sometimes using related anatomical measures such as CMT/CSFT rather than explicitly CRT (PMID: 19643484, PMID: 21499194, PMID: 24903670, PMID: 31918685). Some SUPPORT items are only indirectly relevant because they assess fixed dosing, special subgroups, reviews, or adjunctive combination therapy rather than pure OCT-guided variable-dosing ranibizumab monotherapy, creating some categorization noise (e.g., PMID: 20204659, PMID: 19136707, PMID: 26391465). Overall, the recurring theme is that individualized OCT-guided ranibizumab retreatment generally produces short- to medium-term visual improvement with meaningful retinal thinning while reducing injection burden versus monthly treatment.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence mainly shows that gains from flexible/variable regimens may not be sustained long term or may be inferior to fixed monthly dosing in routine practice. In a large real-world German cohort, an initially improved visual acuity after loading was lost by 12 months under a predominantly visual-acuity-driven flexible retreatment scheme despite reinjections (PMID: 20890777), and a 4-year retrospective series found overall visual acuity declined over time with variable dosing (PMID: 23022167). A 5-year OCT/VA-guided ranibizumab study reported worse mean BCVA and increased central retinal thickness at follow-up, suggesting undertreatment in long-term practice (PMID: 27354758). Other studies in specific subgroups found anatomic improvement without visual acuity improvement or even worsening, such as geographic atrophy-associated CNV, avascular PED, disciform scars, and eyes with good baseline vision (PMID: 23077229, PMID: 19911018, PMID: 19634740, PMID: 25435749). Some CONTRADICT items are not direct refutations of the claim because they compare variable dosing with monthly dosing and show similar rather than worse outcomes, or are review articles summarizing concerns about real-world OCT-guided retreatment (PMID: 21926941, PMID: 23393060).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Multiple directly relevant OCT-guided variable-dosing ranibizumab studies, especially PrONTO and several flexible-regimen cohorts, report improved visual acuity and reduced central retinal thickness, although contradictory real-world and long-term studies indicate these benefits may be less durable and can fall short of monthly dosing when undertreatment occurs.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration treated with intravitreal ranibizumab, the optical coherence tomography-guided variable-dosing regimen achieved visual and anatomic outcomes similar to those seen in phase III studies while requiring fewer intravitreal injections.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the PrONTO study, which explicitly reported that an OCT-guided variable-dosing ranibizumab regimen produced visual acuity and OCT thickness outcomes comparable to phase III trials while using fewer injections over 24 months (mean 9.9 injections) (PMID: 19376495). Additional studies of OCT-/activity-guided PRN or treat-and-extend regimens generally found meaningful visual gains and central retinal thickness reductions with fewer injections than monthly dosing, and several directly reported noninferior or comparable outcomes versus monthly regimens, including TREX-AMD (PMID: 26391465), CANTREAT (PMID: 30677465), and retrospective comparisons of monthly versus variable dosing (PMID: 21926941). Other supportive series also found favorable visual/anatomic outcomes with relatively low injection burden, though many did not explicitly compare results with phase III trials and therefore only indirectly support the claim (PMID: 19217019, PMID: 19730802, PMID: 21459217, PMID: 26200511, PMID: 28449645). Some items in the support set are less directly relevant because they examine treat-and-extend variants, combination therapy, reviews, or protocol descriptions rather than the specific PrONTO-style OCT-guided variable regimen (e.g., PMID: 20204659, PMID: 27009515, PMID: 20463795). Overall, the support side shows a recurring theme that individualized OCT/activity-guided ranibizumab can preserve much of the efficacy of monthly treatment while reducing injections.
contradict_summary: Several real-world and retrospective studies reported that PRN or clinician-determined ranibizumab retreatment achieved worse visual outcomes than randomized trial benchmarks, suggesting that fewer injections may come at the cost of reduced efficacy (PMID: 21135894, PMID: 19477713, PMID: 20937739, PMID: 24424409). Some abstracts specifically linked lower retreatment frequency with poorer maintenance of anatomic improvement or less visual gain, arguing against the idea that reduced-injection regimens reliably match phase III outcomes (PMID: 19643484, PMID: 19289984). One comparative study found standard OCT-guided retreatment protocols yielded smaller mean vision gains than monthly ranibizumab, although loading doses improved outcomes relative to baseline PRN treatment (PMID: 20693484). However, part of the contradiction set is only partially relevant to the claim because it includes atypical subgroups or suboptimal monitoring contexts, such as eyes with geographic atrophy-associated CNV or vitreomacular traction, where poorer outcomes may not reflect the general OCT-guided regimen being claimed (PMID: 23077229, PMID: 24060301). A broader review also noted that real-life physician-dependent OCT-guided retreatment often underperforms clinical trials, reinforcing concerns about undertreatment outside tightly controlled studies (PMID: 23393060).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, mainly because the most directly relevant study—the 2-year PrONTO trial—explicitly concludes that OCT-guided variable dosing achieved phase III-like visual and anatomic outcomes with fewer injections (PMID: 19376495), and multiple later studies echo comparable efficacy with reduced injection burden. However, the contradiction is substantial in real-world practice, where less frequent retreatment and inconsistent OCT monitoring often led to outcomes inferior to trial results, so the claim appears strongest under well-monitored protocolized care rather than routine practice.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration treated with intravitreal ranibizumab, optical coherence tomography was useful for determining when retreatment was needed.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is extensive and repeatedly shows that OCT findings were built directly into ranibizumab retreatment algorithms, especially fluid recurrence and retinal thickness increases, in prospective and retrospective PRN or treat-and-extend studies. The strongest examples are the OCT-guided PrONTO study, which explicitly used OCT criteria for reinjection and achieved favorable 2-year outcomes with fewer injections (PMID: 19376495), and studies showing morphologic recurrences on OCT often preceded symptoms, VA loss, Amsler changes, or fundus findings, supporting OCT for early retreatment decisions (PMID: 21768186). Randomized and large-study data also suggest added value of OCT-based retreatment versus functional criteria alone or show that retreatment decisions are mainly driven by OCT markers such as intraretinal/subretinal fluid (PMID: 31918685, PMID: 32066898). Additional abstracts report that SD-OCT detects subtle residual or recurrent fluid missed by clinical exam or older OCT methods and can support individualized intervals, prediction of retreatment burden, and even omission of some ancillary exams in selected cases (PMID: 24324880, PMID: 22733165, PMID: 31735634). Some supporting items are only indirect or include combination therapy rather than ranibizumab monotherapy, but the overall abstract-level pattern strongly favors OCT as useful for determining retreatment need.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mostly challenges OCT-only or specific quantitative OCT thresholds rather than the overall usefulness of OCT. One study found that in routine care, quantitative OCT retreatment criteria such as a CRT increase of at least 100 μm were insufficiently sensitive, with only 44.4% of recurrences meeting that threshold, whereas VA criteria were more sensitive (PMID: 24903670). Another study reported that resolution of fluid on OCT did not correlate with the degree of visual improvement, and more frequent injections were associated with better vision, suggesting OCT-defined dryness alone may not optimally guide treatment intensity (PMID: 19643484). Additional routine-care reports mainly show low OCT use in practice and associated undertreatment or poor outcomes, which is more a health-services problem than direct evidence against OCT usefulness (PMID: 24424409, PMID: 24838864). There is therefore some tension around relying on OCT alone or rigid OCT thresholds, but little direct abstract-level evidence that OCT is not useful at all.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Across many studies, OCT was explicitly used to detect disease activity and trigger ranibizumab retreatment, whereas the opposing evidence mainly argues that OCT alone or certain OCT thresholds may be insufficient, not that OCT lacks usefulness.

claim: In patients with subfoveal choroidal neovascularization secondary to age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab treatment improved visual acuity more than sham injection.
support_summary: Strong direct evidence comes from large randomized, double-masked sham-controlled trials showing ranibizumab improved visual acuity versus sham in neovascular/subfoveal AMD-related CNV. In MARINA, mean visual acuity increased by 6.5 to 7.2 letters with ranibizumab versus a 10.4-letter decrease with sham at 12 months, with benefits maintained to 24 months (PMID: 17021318), and subgroup analyses found ranibizumab superior to sham across all evaluated subgroups (PMID: 17270674). In PIER, 24-month visual acuity losses were much smaller with ranibizumab than sham (-2.2/-2.3 vs -21.4 letters; P<0.0001) (PMID: 20598667), and post hoc MARINA analyses similarly reported visual acuity gains with ranibizumab but deterioration with sham even in eyes developing macular atrophy (PMID: 37086257). Additional support comes from pooled analyses/meta-analysis showing marked superiority over control/sham arms (PMID: 31562118, PMID: 32953000), though several listed supportive items are only indirect because they compare ranibizumab with verteporfin/usual care or are reviews/observational studies rather than sham-controlled trials (e.g., PMID: 19118696, PMID: 17021319, PMID: 16483659).
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is weak and largely not directly applicable to the claim population. A randomized trial of prophylactic ranibizumab in fellow eyes with intermediate AMD found no visual acuity benefit versus sham, but these eyes did not have subfoveal CNV secondary to AMD, so it does not directly test the claim (PMID: 35121216). A retrospective real-world cohort reported stabilization rather than clear visual acuity improvement over 6 months and explicitly noted outcomes were less favorable than phase III studies, but it lacked a sham comparator and therefore cannot directly contradict the sham-controlled claim (PMID: 20336600).
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Multiple high-quality sham-controlled randomized trials directly in patients with subfoveal/neovascular AMD-related CNV consistently show ranibizumab produces substantially better visual acuity outcomes than sham, whereas the contradicting studies are indirect or in different populations.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration–related subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, ranibizumab reduced choroidal neovascularization growth and leakage compared with sham injection.
support_summary: Direct support from primary clinical data comes mainly from a phase I/II controlled trial in neovascular AMD with subfoveal predominantly or minimally classic CNV, where repeated ranibizumab injections were associated with decreased fluorescein leakage and reduced subretinal fluid versus usual care, along with improved visual acuity (PMID: 16483659). Several review articles state that ranibizumab/anti-VEGF therapy inhibits CNV activity, reduces vascular permeability or leakage, and is effective for neovascular AMD, but these are indirect for the specific sham-controlled claim and generally do not report the exact comparison of ranibizumab versus sham on CNV growth/leakage from original trial data (PMID: 22911433, PMID: 22174998, PMID: 22346114, PMID: 21654887, PMID: 25837316, PMID: 26804762). One item in the support set is misaligned with the claim because it studies stereotactic radiotherapy added to PRN ranibizumab rather than ranibizumab versus sham injection, so it should be treated as only loosely relevant evidence (PMID: 23490327). Overall, the supportive evidence is thematically consistent that ranibizumab reduces neovascular activity and leakage, but direct abstract-level evidence for the exact sham comparison is limited in this evidence set.
contradict_summary: The lone contradicting item is a post hoc MARINA analysis focused on macular atrophy, not the primary efficacy comparison of CNV growth/leakage; it reports more newly detectable macular atrophy in ranibizumab-treated eyes than sham but also notes ranibizumab-treated eyes had clinically significant visual acuity gains while sham-treated eyes worsened (PMID: 37086257). Its statement that no association was found between detectable macular atrophy and change in leakage/CNV area at 24 months does not directly show that ranibizumab failed to reduce CNV growth or leakage versus sham. Thus, this paper is only a weak and somewhat discrepant contradiction to the claim as phrased.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest relevant evidence in this set shows ranibizumab decreased leakage and improved lesion-related outcomes in neovascular AMD, while the only contradicting study is a secondary analysis centered on macular atrophy and does not directly refute reduced CNV growth/leakage versus sham.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration–related subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, ranibizumab was well tolerated and had a low incidence of serious ocular or nonocular adverse events compared with sham injection.
support_summary: Direct sham-controlled evidence supports the claim: in the MARINA trial, ranibizumab was associated with low serious ocular event rates over 24 months (presumed endophthalmitis 1.0%, serious uveitis 1.3%) and was concluded to have low rates of serious adverse events versus sham in AMD-related subfoveal CNV (PMID: 17021318). The 2-year PIER sham-controlled study also reported a favorable ocular safety profile consistent with prior reports (PMID: 20598667). Additional non-sham studies and extensions consistently describe ranibizumab as safe/well tolerated with low rates of serious ocular and systemic events across large cohorts and longer follow-up, including SAILOR, ANCHOR, HORIZON, SUSTAIN, SECURE, and LUMINOUS, although these are indirect for the sham comparison (PMID: 19643495, PMID: 19118696, PMID: 22306121, PMID: 21459217, PMID: 23021093, PMID: 23850682, PMID: 31764612). One listed “supporting” item is only preclinical nanomicelle work and is not relevant to the patient-level sham comparison, indicating some evidence-set noise/discrepancy (PMID: 39281109).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited but notable. In FOCUS, monthly ranibizumab plus PDT versus sham injection plus PDT improved efficacy but increased serious intraocular inflammation (11.4%) and had endophthalmitis rates of 1.9% (or 4.8% including presumed cases), which conflicts with a simple characterization of uniformly low serious ocular adverse events compared with sham injection (PMID: 17101999). A pooled randomized-trial analysis found overall 2-year cerebrovascular accident rates below 3%, but suggested some, though not definitive, evidence of increased CVA risk with ranibizumab versus control in high-risk patients, raising concern about serious nonocular events (PMID: 23011184). However, both contradictory studies are indirect to the exact statement because one evaluates ranibizumab combined with PDT and the other reports a pooled safety signal rather than a direct sham-comparison tolerability result for the specific trial/population.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from direct sham-controlled ranibizumab trials in AMD-related subfoveal CNV showing favorable safety and low serious adverse-event rates, while the contradictory evidence is limited, somewhat indirect, and largely reflects specific concerns (notably inflammation in the ranibizumab+PDT setting and a possible CVA signal in high-risk patients).

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, intravitreal ranibizumab provided better visual acuity outcomes than verteporfin photodynamic therapy, with more patients maintaining or improving vision and fewer patients losing vision.
support_summary: The strongest evidence comes from the ANCHOR randomized trial and related analyses, which directly enrolled patients with predominantly classic neovascular AMD and found ranibizumab clearly superior to verteporfin PDT: at 12 months, 94.3% to 96.4% of ranibizumab-treated patients lost <15 letters versus 64.3% with verteporfin, 35.7% to 40.3% gained >=15 letters versus 5.6%, and mean visual acuity improved with ranibizumab but declined with verteporfin (PMID: 17021319). A subgroup analysis of ANCHOR reported ranibizumab outperformed PDT across all evaluated subgroups for letter loss, letter gain, and mean VA change (PMID: 17949673), and patient-reported functional outcomes and driving-related outcomes also favored ranibizumab over PDT in ANCHOR-based analyses (PMID: 19139332, PMID: 23009891). Additional supportive but less direct evidence includes FOCUS, where ranibizumab+PDT beat PDT alone in predominantly classic CNV (PMID: 17101999, PMID: 18321465), and several studies/meta-analyses showing ranibizumab monotherapy often outperformed ranibizumab+PDT combination therapy, indirectly suggesting PDT adds limited benefit or may worsen VA outcomes (PMID: 22424834, PMID: 22444829, PMID: 21124254, PMID: 25787216, PMID: 24967206). Some supporting items are indirect, non-comparative, cost-effectiveness, review, or non-AMD/non-classic CNV studies and therefore weaker or less specific to the claim (e.g., PMID: 19940805, PMID: 22911433, PMID: 23219069, PMID: 30077969), but they generally align with the main RCT finding.
contradict_summary: Only one contradicting item was provided, and it does not directly compare ranibizumab monotherapy against PDT alone; instead it compares ranibizumab monotherapy with ranibizumab plus single-session PDT and concludes both were similarly effective in reducing CNV activity morphologically (PMID: 27739558). Because the abstract emphasizes retreatment indications and morphologic outcomes rather than the classic claim outcomes of maintaining/improving vision versus losing vision, its relevance as contradiction is limited. This creates a discrepancy: the study is categorized as contradicting, but from the abstract it does not directly refute the claim that ranibizumab is superior to verteporfin PDT alone in predominantly classic subfoveal CNV.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This judgment is driven by direct high-quality randomized evidence in the target population from ANCHOR showing substantially better visual acuity outcomes with ranibizumab than verteporfin PDT, whereas the lone contradicting study is indirect and does not truly test the same comparison.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, intravitreal ranibizumab produced more favorable retinal lesion anatomic changes than verteporfin photodynamic therapy.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from studies comparing ranibizumab-containing regimens with verteporfin PDT alone in predominantly classic neovascular AMD, where ranibizumab was associated with less lesion growth, greater reduction in CNV leakage, and less subretinal fluid accumulation over 2 years (PMID: 18321465). The ANCHOR trial itself primarily reported superior visual acuity outcomes for ranibizumab versus verteporfin in predominantly classic subfoveal CNV (PMID: 17021319), and a subgroup analysis confirmed superiority across clinical subgroups but did not directly detail lesion anatomy in the abstract (PMID: 17949673). Additional but indirect support comes from combination-treatment studies showing better angiographic or OCT-type morphologic outcomes than PDT alone, including reduced leakage in the FOCUS and monkey studies (PMID: 18321465, PMID: 15824225), and from DENALI where ranibizumab monotherapy showed greater central retinal thickness reduction than ranibizumab+PDT, though this is not a direct ranibizumab-versus-PDT-alone comparison (PMID: 22444829). Several other cited studies mainly address visual acuity or cost-effectiveness rather than anatomic lesion changes, so they only weakly support the specific claim (PMID: 17101999, PMID: 19940805, PMID: 20349399, PMID: 23751929).
contradict_summary: The only contradicting study did not compare ranibizumab monotherapy with verteporfin PDT alone; instead, it compared ranibizumab monotherapy versus ranibizumab plus a single PDT session and found similar morphologic improvements in leakage area, intraretinal cysts, and subretinal fluid in both groups (PMID: 27739558). This means it does not directly contradict the claim about ranibizumab versus verteporfin PDT alone in new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal CNV, but it does suggest that adding PDT to ranibizumab may not further improve morphologic outcomes. Thus, the contradiction category contains a study whose population/comparator do not cleanly match the claim, creating a notable discrepancy.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The best-matched evidence includes direct reports of more favorable lesion anatomy with ranibizumab-containing treatment than PDT alone, while the lone contradictory item is not a true head-to-head ranibizumab-versus-PDT-alone study and therefore only weakly challenges the claim.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, ranibizumab and verteporfin photodynamic therapy had similarly low rates of serious ocular and nonocular adverse events.
support_summary: Several later studies comparing ranibizumab-based regimens with verteporfin PDT-containing regimens in neovascular AMD reported broadly similar safety profiles and low ocular serious adverse event rates, including MONT BLANC (comparable safety profiles, low ocular serious adverse events; PMID: 22424834), DENALI (all regimens well tolerated, ocular serious adverse events low and mostly single cases; PMID: 22444829), and a small randomized study reporting no serious adverse events in either combined therapy or ranibizumab-alone groups (PMID: 23362390). Multiple small open-label or pilot combination studies also described no ocular/systemic adverse events or only rare serious events not considered treatment related, supporting the general impression that verteporfin-ranibizumab use was well tolerated (PMID: 20204659, PMID: 18667951, PMID: 18780651, PMID: 19878113, PMID: 18779720, PMID: 18772178). However, most of these supportive studies evaluated combination therapy versus ranibizumab alone or were uncontrolled combination series, not the exact head-to-head ranibizumab-versus-verteporfin comparison in new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal CNV. Thus, they mainly provide indirect support for the claim's assertion of low serious ocular/nonocular adverse event rates.
contradict_summary: The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from pivotal predominantly classic neovascular AMD trials. In the FOCUS year-1 and year-2 reports, ranibizumab plus PDT had higher serious ocular adverse event rates than PDT alone, including serious intraocular inflammation (11.4%-12.4%) and endophthalmitis/presumed endophthalmitis, while serious nonocular adverse events were similar between groups (PMID: 17101999, PMID: 18321465). This contradicts the idea of similarly low serious ocular adverse event rates across ranibizumab- and verteporfin-based treatment arms, at least for the combination regimen versus PDT alone. One listed contradiction study, ANCHOR, is actually mixed from the abstract: it reports low serious ocular adverse events with ranibizumab (PMID: 17021319) but does not clearly show verteporfin had higher serious ocular or nonocular adverse event rates in the abstract, so its placement as contradiction is less secure and represents a categorization discrepancy.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is somewhat more contradicted than supported. Although many later studies suggest low and broadly comparable safety across ranibizumab- and verteporfin-containing regimens, the most direct randomized evidence in predominantly classic CNV shows higher serious ocular adverse events in ranibizumab+PDT versus PDT alone, while nonocular serious events appear similar.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an OCT-guided variable-dosing regimen with intravitreal ranibizumab improved visual acuity and reduced retinal thickness over 2 years.
support_summary: Several studies report that ranibizumab given with individualized/OCT-guided retreatment improves vision and reduces retinal thickness in neovascular AMD, including 24-month data showing significant BCVA gains and CRT/CMT reductions with relatively few injections (PMID: 19217019), and the original OCT-guided variable-dosing PrONTO study showing strong 12-month improvement with a 2-year study design but only 12-month outcomes in the abstract (PMID: 17386270). Additional 2-year or near-2-year real-world/retrospective series using PRN or treat-and-extend strategies also found mean visual acuity gains and central retinal thickness decreases, such as treat-and-extend ranibizumab with improved vision through 2 years (PMID: 20591490, PMID: 28449645) and other 24-month cohorts with significant BCVA and CMT/CRT improvement (PMID: 20224469, PMID: 19951942). Some evidence is indirect because it involves combination therapy with PDT plus ranibizumab rather than ranibizumab alone (PMID: 20204659), or reviews/observational analyses that support OCT-guided monitoring without directly testing the exact 2-year regimen in the claim (PMID: 19668384, PMID: 27197868). Overall, the recurring theme is that individualized ranibizumab retreatment based on OCT/activity criteria is associated with maintained or improved vision and reduced retinal thickness, although several supporting items are not exact matches to the specific 2-year OCT-guided monotherapy claim.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence mainly comes from studies where ranibizumab reduced retinal thickness but did not significantly improve standard visual acuity, including short-term treatment of exudative disciform scars with no VA change despite OCT improvement (PMID: 19634740) and a 2-year PRN study with significant CRT reduction but no statistically significant BCVA improvement from baseline (PMID: 24184931). Other long-term observational studies of OCT-guided/PRN anti-VEGF care reported gradual VA decline over 5-6 years and, in one study, increased central retinal thickness over time, suggesting undertreatment or real-world limitations rather than clear failure of early 2-year response (PMID: 27354758, PMID: 29789898). A review also noted that real-world physician-dependent OCT-guided retreatment often yields outcomes inferior to clinical trials, but this is indirect rather than a direct test of the claim (PMID: 23393060). Thus, the main contradictory theme is that anatomical benefit does not always translate into visual acuity gain, especially in chronic scarred disease or less intensive real-world management.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple studies, including direct 24-month ranibizumab variable-dosing cohorts, show improved visual acuity together with reduced retinal thickness, whereas contradictory evidence is largely indirect, involves different populations or longer-term undertreated real-world settings, or shows anatomical improvement without clear VA gain.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an OCT-guided variable-dosing regimen with intravitreal ranibizumab achieved visual outcomes comparable with those reported in phase III ranibizumab trials while requiring fewer intravitreal injections.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the PrONTO study itself, which reported that after 3 loading doses, an OCT-guided variable ranibizumab regimen in neovascular AMD improved mean visual acuity by 9.3 letters at 12 months with only 5.6 injections, and explicitly concluded outcomes were similar to phase III trials with fewer injections (PMID: 17386270). Several additional studies of individualized, OCT- or activity-guided ranibizumab regimens found similar visual outcomes with fewer injections than monthly dosing, including retrospective monthly-vs-variable comparisons (PMID: 21926941), OCT-based retreatment series (PMID: 19730802), and prospective/randomized treat-and-extend trials showing noninferior vision with reduced injection burden (PMID: 26391465, PMID: 30677465, PMID: 31917441). Other real-world or prospective series also reported meaningful gains with relatively low injection counts, often describing outcomes as comparable to pivotal ranibizumab trials or monthly dosing (PMID: 24637667, PMID: 19648888, PMID: 26200511, PMID: 21459217). A recurring theme is that individualized retreatment based on OCT/clinical activity can preserve much of the visual benefit while reducing injections and visits. However, many supporting items are not exact matches to the claim because they study treat-and-extend, general PRN, mixed anti-VEGF regimens, or broader real-world practice rather than the specific OCT-guided PrONTO-style regimen, so their support is indirect.
contradict_summary: Several real-world reports found that reduced-frequency or clinician-determined ranibizumab retreatment did not reproduce trial-level visual outcomes, despite relatively low injection numbers: one Canadian retrospective study reported only +2.9 letters overall and concluded outcomes compared poorly with trials (PMID: 21135894), and a prospective cohort with clinician-determined retreatment found BCVA improvement was less than in randomized trials (PMID: 20937739). A review article similarly stated that real-life outcomes of physician-dependent OCT-guided retreatment are inferior to clinical trial outcomes (PMID: 23393060). Observational AURA analyses suggested maintaining or markedly improving vision may require higher injection frequencies than often delivered in practice, implying that fewer injections can compromise outcomes (PMID: 27197868). One listed contradiction study is only a head-to-head comparison of conbercept vs ranibizumab under TREX and does not directly test the claim about phase III comparability of an OCT-guided ranibizumab regimen, so its placement is a discrepancy (PMID: 28937147).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The direct evidence from the PrONTO abstract (PMID: 17386270) matches the claim closely and is reinforced by multiple later studies showing individualized ranibizumab regimens can achieve similar vision outcomes with fewer injections, although substantial real-world evidence indicates that this is not consistently achieved outside tightly managed protocols.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration and central-fovea neovascularization, repeated intravitreal ranibizumab treatment produced sustained anatomic and functional benefit over 2 years.
support_summary: Multiple randomized trials and large observational cohorts report that repeated intravitreal ranibizumab can preserve or improve vision through 24 months in neovascular/subfoveal AMD, with many studies also showing sustained reductions in retinal thickness or fluid; key examples include MARINA (benefit maintained at 24 months, PMID: 17021318), PIER year 2 (ranibizumab superior to sham at month 24, PMID: 20598667), HARBOR 24-month results (clinically meaningful BCVA gains at month 24, PMID: 25015215), and several real-world 2-year series showing improved/stable BCVA plus reduced CRT/CMT (PMID: 19217019, PMID: 19951942, PMID: 23620657, PMID: 28449645).
